Welcome to Magnetic Marketing, session number four, the little known secrets for generating a flood of highly qualified leads. In this session, Dan will discuss the most effective ways to generate leads and a little bit about the different media that you can use. Okay, so Dan, what exactly is this lead generation thing? It is a broader and more comprehensive approach to marketing than almost everybody engages in. So almost everybody engages in get a customer. Right. Which at any given time excludes a significant percentage of the people who you communicated with, you spent money communicating with, who have some level of interest. And so lead generation is about capturing that secondary quantity of people who can be developed into customers, clients, patients, donors, through a series of steps over a period of time. So leads are people who have raised their hands, so they have stepped forward, they have raised their hands, and they have given permission. They have moved you from a knowing past to welcome guests, and they have given permission to now tell me more, communicate with me. Right. Right. So lead generation, when you were single in a bar, is can I buy you a drink? Right. Right? No, I'm with somebody. Game over. Right. Yes, raised hand and gave a certain amount of permission now to have a conversation. Got it. And the reality is that you're leaving a whole bunch of money on the table, a lot of marketing dollars on the table if you don't lead generate, aren't you? Huge sums. And you are leaving yourself a business dependent upon random activity rather than control. So if we take the Buick ad and the Super Bowl, Right. Buicks could spend next dollars, they're gonna run those commercials, and they hope that some number of people will now come look at Buick who might only have looked at Honda and Ford before. Right. Who are sorta close to buying a car. A direct response marketer would want to capture as many of the names and addresses and email addresses and all of that of those people, and in fact a bigger number of people who are a year away from shopping for a car. Right. And have a dialogue with them so that through now a process they can maybe squeeze Ford and Honda out and they can get the person to focus on Buick as a preference or a first priority or first choice or do a better job of pre-selling them before they get to the dealership so that they do get the first presentation close them and they don't go competitively shop. Got it. That's the difference. Okay, so again it's getting people to raise their hand and say hey I have some interest in what you have to offer. So let's, we define what lead generation actually is. Can you give us examples of some different lead generation models that people can follow? Yeah, so if you, the easiest thing for people to see is to look at the big direct response advertisers who do television and do radio and do print. And so in gardeners category that would be sleep number beds. So sleep number beds is a phenomenal example for people to pay attention to. I don't recommend the bed, you may now issue a disclaimer. But, but- It's a fine, fine bed. But there are fine example to pay attention to because first of all they're a schizophrenic company in that they are a national direct response marketer who actually does sell beds direct to consumer through sending packages of information through driving to the web, but they also have 300 and some out retail stores. That same marketing drives people too. And they do national advertising and then they do local market by market by market advertising. Most of their local market by market by market advertising is more like normal advertising. It's we gotta store, it's product service, features benefits, price discount, right? We gotta store. In it we got beds. They're better beds. Here's the features of them, the main one of which is there's a do-it-you-can-use in this side inflates and this side deflates. And here's the benefits of that, right? And this weekend we gotta sale and you get a free pillow. And so their local advertising is better crafted but it's not much different than pretty much any other furniture, bed or mattress store you might see in your market. Now if you pay attention to their national advertising you will see the other side of the company. Cause their direct, their national advertising is, oh by the way we got stores, but raise your hand, let us send you a free information packet with a thing about how to get a better night's sleep, a literature packet all about the beds, a shopping guide, a DVD with testimonials on it of people who sleep on the beds, let us send you this package, right? And it includes a voucher. And so in their category there's a big national marketer of sunrooms. There's a big national marketer of blinds that go over your backyard deck, right? And they feed the lead to a local dealer who now gets a sales rep into your home to make that sale. But the start of their process is let us send you this stuff. So the offer is this stuff that will make you a better informed consumer, may have some benefit about how to get a better night's sleep whether you ever buy a mattress from us or not. But will definitely make you a better informed consumer. We'll help you not make a bad decision, we'll help you make a good decision and it's free and we wanna send it to you, right? Now they really don't wanna send it to you. They really want you to give them permission and get you into a sales process or a funnel is what we call it. And so this is about exchange for name, address, email, phone, in some cases a little data and permission. That's what you see. And so if you look at sleep number beds, if you look at the name of the on-and-guys, it escapes me, but if you look at the on-and-guys, if you look at premier bathtubs, Bowflex, if you look at financial Fisher investments. So there was a week before we did this, full page ads in USA Today by Fisher, they're often in Forbes and financial magazines and so forth, but every once in a while, he broadens the scope. And so the full page ads in USA Today, in almost every category of product or service, there is at least one big national direct response advertiser, so that's who you can look at. Now if you wanna look at even smarter ones, then you look at ones that are in the GKSE world. Who are in here? Because they will be doing it even better than these folks are doing it. But if you just wanna, if you click the dial tonight and watch a couple of hours of television and you get off ABC, NBC, CBS and Fox, and you get onto cable stations and onto local markets, you won't go an hour without seeing this kind of advertising. If you listen to syndicated radio, you won't go 30 minutes without hearing this kind of advertising. And then the next smart thing to do if you want free self-education is answer the ads and play prospect. Yeah, and see how they follow up and everything like that. You know, one of the huge advantages of lead generation, aside from the obvious one though, is if you're sending the right materials, and we'll get into that in just a little bit, you're also doing a really good job of pre-selling someone before you even have to talk to them, isn't that true? Yeah, and you accomplish a couple of things. We actually accomplish three important things. One is you actually make the buying process less stressful for the prospect to patient, prospect to customer, prospect to client, right? Because our normal buying process is get you into our store, showroom, clinic or office. It has quickly after you raise your hand as humanly possible, right? Thereby you've had the least amount of information possible. And then we sort of brute force sell you. And in many cases we have the idea that if we don't get you now, we'll never get you. So we either get you or leave you bleeding and unhappy and dead on the floor, cause no harm, no foul, right? Well that's very stressful and unpleasant for the buyer. And again, some percentage of them will still buy, but there's a difference between a buyer and a customer and a customer who becomes a champion and all that. So you do a lot of value damage that way that can be erased when we again create attraction and we let them come to us, okay? So the lowest threshold offer in the world is let me send you or in some cases come to the web and see, free information that will make you smarter about this thing that you are interested in, right? Very low hurdle, very low stress, right? So it changes that dynamic. Secondly, it allows you to change your positioning from another seller of stuff to a expert or helpful advisor about things, right? And third, it takes the dependence off of being, having to be, and if you grow, having to employ really great killer sales people, right? And so if you do it all yourself and you're spectacular, right? So obviously I can get a check out of a rock, right? I mean, so you put me, pretty much in front of somebody who could possibly buy, I know how to beat them to death and get there. I can do that, right? But can I clone me? Well, that's not so easy. Can I multiply me? So yourself is easy, maybe a second one, maybe a third one, 30, not so easy. 300, impossible. So the best sales person in his presentation, the best chiropractor or dentist and his case presentation report of findings, the best lawyer in his case presentation of why we should do your trust documents, the best disintegrates rapidly when he tries to replicate it, right? And therefore the results go down, because it was dependent on being great at it, having no emotional hangups about it, having all of the stuff that really makes a spectacular sales person. This allows you to get great results with barely good sales people. It reduces the reliance on them being great. See, so even in a store, single sales people, so even in a store, single store, so they would tell you that pretty much either one of them, Jeff or Ben, will have a higher closing average than anybody who works for them, right? To try and fix it. To hire and fire and hire and fire. Trade, oh, you should brutalize and yell out and incentivize and stick to eating dinner every Friday and all the stuff you do to try and get those other five guys to perform like you perform. It's not really the most effective solution to how do we best monetize every prospect. It's a process that allows somebody not as good as they are to get that. And the other thing your lead generation could do is set up the buying criteria as well. What the prospect should be looking for when they're thinking about buying. It's such a, go ahead. Yeah, it's such a clever thing. Yeah, this is little advanced now, but it's good. Yeah, it is advanced. But when you bring somebody directly to a sales event, right, so I see it, sleep number TV ad and I get in the car and I go to the sleep number bed store. First of all, odds are I'm gonna go to four or five stores not one all on the same Saturday, so that's a problem. But I come in that store with no way to analyze, evaluate and make choices and say, I have this big idea. Also, you sit here in this audience and you see this done today and for your business, you decide you want to replicate this and you're gonna go home to wherever you're from and you're gonna now go for a business, you know, and produce to do it. And there's eight. You have no new criteria to figure out what you the audience and the absence of any good convention. You'll probably just have to find a school to do the worst thing in the second. But you will have no organized help in how to evaluate and make a decision. And most people are not very good or confident about making decisions, which makes that all the worse. When we generally lead and communicate with them, educate them, well, more of a decision is said as a decision. We can give them back that set of questions, that criteria. You can give them a tool, you can give them a chart, a graph, 20 questions, five dollars a check and we can train the process so it favors us as well. So we can create control, the way they make a decision and we can control it in a way that favors us and disfavors our competition. Yeah, absolutely. And I mean, it's really good to see what a lead generation back, what a lead generation back in such business that the tankers have reported. The seventh thing you must ask any entertainer before you hire a beer company back. And I said to my criteria, one of them was that they guarantee their performance well, guess what? I was the only one that guaranteed it. So the meeting plan literally, they would tell me they would take my report, they would go through the seventh criteria, ask the other entertainer, well, do you guarantee your performance? Now we don't, oh, okay. I have that for virtual effort and again, we'll find the most serious students, yes, and others, right? Who will generate leads and deliver information. We will finally be carrying some sort of document like that. So Matt, who's coming from Australia and New Zealand and is speaking at the GKIC Super Conference, what's his last name, I should know it. Matt, the back air patio guy, the tropical patio guy, Book Dole, Buchel. Oh, Matt, from the guy in the UK. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, from Australia. What's his name, Durs? Matt, Buchel. Australia, Buchel. Buchel, yeah. Matt has that document and he sells backyard, patio, thatched roof, tropical things, gazebos. So everybody gets, when they're packaging materials, they request, they get their multi-page, how to plan a backyard and how to pick the right gazebo and how to pick the right provider. Well, now somebody, he's the only one that's provided that. So if somebody is gonna go to four or five of them, if they're gonna use a tool at all, they're gonna use his tool because nobody else gave them a tool. That's right. Right? Right. And even if they don't walk around with it, they now have it in their head, right, at least. So it's a part of a lead generation bag that offer that people instinctively, yeah, I'd like to have that. Right, absolutely. Before I pick a financial advisor, I'd love to know 23 smart questions to ask every financial advisor before I give them all my money, right? And if you're the only financial advisor who's offered to give me the questions, well, yours are the only questions I got. That's right. And if you've structured them so that they favor you, there you go. Very powerful. Again, a little bit of advanced strategy, which was not in the original magnetic marketing, but something that you should take indefinitely. So we've talked about this lead generation magnet. What is it? Well, so conceptually, a lead generation magnet is what makes people respond to a lead generation advertisement, right? So there has to be something that your target person wants for which they are willing to step forward, raise their hand, give you contact information, and give you some level of permission to continue a dialogue with them, right? So the great lead generation ads are, I see you have it in front of you. I don't know when you're getting to it, but the great lead generation ads are basically personal's ad structured, right? So they are about who I want, who I don't want. And in most cases, now the lead generation magnet is implicit, right? It's if you, because I've described me. So if you want me, and you don't have any of these things I don't want, okay, so that's a lead generation magnet. Now, in most commercial lead generation applications, you aren't enough, right? Or you're premature, so we need stuff that they'll want. Often these are information based lead generation magnets. We just described one. So it might be a book, it might be a DVD, it might be a full information package, it might be a video they can see online, it might be a thing they can download online. The online deliverables have the obvious disadvantage of giving no reason or justification to demand your full physical address, right? They have the advantage of lower threshold, right? So books often used, okay? Or reports go in that category, book or report. So again, gardener says information, what's the title of your main thing? What's keeping you up at night? How to get a good night's sleep, you know that, okay? So it's not how to buy a mattress, right? It's broader and suggests value whether I ever buy a mattress from you or not, okay? So, and there's just a booklet, it could be a book. Batsagool and I entered the financial advisor niche several years ago, it was a free book on trust, right? It can be a DVD, so Perillo too, often DVDs only make sense when there's a reason for it to be a DVD. So whether there's some type of demonstration. Some type of demonstration. So Perillo Tours offers a DVD because you can see the four countries and the boat and everything that you're gonna go do, right? So that makes perfect sense, right? And audio CD often used. If you listen to direct response radio, probably 30% of the offers are for a audio CD or include a audio CD. One of our long time GKIC members, Ron Legrand, if you listen to satellite radio, you will hear Ron right now. All of you won't go an hour and you will hear Ron, hi this is Ron Legrand. Have you ever thought about investing in real estate but you don't know how that's the start of the commercial? I can't do the rest of it by heart. But its end is, let us give you a free CD. You have the big information package offer like sleep number bed, right? Hair club for men now Bosley bought them so Bosley, Premier Bat Tubs, Disney vacations. Cause again, let's send you a whole thing. Grocers, DVD, vacation planner, all this cool stuff, right? Free video online. So Mars who's managing us has worked with Agora here. So theendofamerica.com, you're going to see this free video message, right? We have a, one of my clients, one of our offline to online's is how to get a second paycheck without a second job, how to earn finders fees at home with your computer. That's postcard driven to go see a free video, right, at a website. So that's the lead generation magnet. Is you're going to go see this cool free video that tells you the secrets of this promise we just made. So often it's information, sometimes it's not. Sometimes it's fun, it's a bribe. So sometimes it's information plus bribe. Often when they feel it's necessary for it to be a bribe, sometimes it's information plus cool stuff. So not only do you get this information, but you get a free coffee mug with a patriotic saying on it. Right, right. And so there's a little extra juice to it. The key also with this and a big mistake that people make when they're doing their lead generation is they mistake that they're selling their product or their service as opposed to quote selling whether it's free or not, selling the magnet. When you are doing lead generation and magnetic marketing as a whole, you are dealing with processes, not with one shot selling. So this backs up to at the point that I want you raise your hand, express interest, and give me contact information and permission. All I am selling is the lead generation magnet that you want enough to make that exchange with me. Anything else I do gets in the way. Even brand gets in the way. Now some companies won't sacrifice brand for greater response. Often for all sorts of reasons we talked about segment one. So I can take, in many cases, I can take a national advertiser. So let's take Disney. I can take Disney out of their vacation lead generation advertising, do it blind and get higher response. But Disney is not gonna sacrifice the brand building for the higher response because they're a public corporation, they got shareholders, they got egotistical people running it, they got all that. We don't have that handicap so we can try both ways. But it is about doing nothing but getting you to agree to the exchange of the items I need in exchange for this pile of stuff. Anything else you do is diluting the effectiveness of that message. And you have to think long and hard about anything else that's in there. Is this affecting adversely my actual goal? Then that stuff, its primary job, and you gotta be very careful about getting in its way, is now getting people to agree to a sales event, in most cases. So that's sell the appointment. Now whether that's a phone appointment, a web appointment, a coming to the showroom, coming to the office, whatever. So we gotta be careful not to get ahead of ourselves there. And so every one of these things fits a process. And most marketers will try and do too much all at the same time. And thereby jeopardize the primary thing that we're trying to do. Just so you know, in here there is campaigns that include the lead generation aspect of it, but then also what you send to the person once they've raised their hand and given you the information. So there's a model in here for the free report. So this is what a free report would look like. There's 17 things that we were talking about. So there's one of those in here as well that you can take and model. Dan, you mentioned this, this is a classic. Oh yeah, Halbert's ad. From the late great Gary Halbert. Talk a little bit about this. We're gonna put, this is gonna be, this is in the resource section. Because it's a classic. So it's a full page ad. It's actually a full page ad. Busted it up into pages. This is one big full page newspaper ad, which a great copywriter no longer with us, Gary Halbert, ran. So it's a full page newspaper ad that is a personal's ad. This is an ad he ran to find a woman. And it probably is the best copy he ever wrote. You could teach from it for days. Absolutely. You should study it closely. And it is very much, here's what I am, here's what I want, here's what I don't want. It is very much that. Now it is done in a particular style, but nonetheless. So it starts, you know, generous creative businessman wants to find a hot, sexy woman with a good sense of humor. Well that's sort of, that's not beyond the realm of what we might find on in personal's ads of old or match.com right now. From there though it gets. It gets. You gotta read it. No, I'm not. No, no, no, I'm saying, no, no. I'm saying you have to read it. You have to read it. The owner's a magnetic marketing. So in the middle, for example, it starts seven things Gary does not want from a woman. And from here to here. So I don't know, fourth of the page, when you re-format this as a newspaper ad, is a descriptions of the seven things Gary does not want from a woman. They're very funny too. There is a description of profiles of women he does not want. Sherry the tragic, Karen the would-be prostitute, Claudia the actress. So there is, number seven is really brilliant because it holds out implicit benefit. So number seven is I don't want a woman who can't stand prosperity. It's particularly brilliant because it says, I have money. I got money without saying I've got money. It's an absolute brilliant ad. The only means of response by the way was in writing. But there is a strong call to action. Yeah. And several times I saw a good deal of what he had kept that came in from it, which were boxes and boxes and boxes of equally long and elaborate responses, ranging everything from, you know, ill-usable trailer park trash to vice president of a bank. To, I mean, to really quality respondents and everything in between. Some very interesting photographs. Those are not included in your system. And it did incidentally produce a wonderful lady with whom Halbert had a significant other relationship for probably a decade, maybe 11 or 12 years. And it is absolutely a terrific lead generation ad. Right, yeah, absolutely. So we talked about this was in the newspaper. Let's talk about it. A lot of people ask, so how do I figure out the best media to use to start generating? But before I get to that question, I've got another question, which I don't have here, which is a really important one. Is there a way that you can combine both, have a lead generation element and a direct response element? So let's say I'm a dentist, right? I'm placing my ad. I want to capture the patient who is ready to come in now. In some cases, how do I, is it possible to do both? Yeah, so there's three basic choices, right, in all of this. We can be lead generation only. And very often in those cases, we are trying to screen just as he did. So my wealth advisor who now advertises in Forbes and Cowboys and Indians and so forth, who only wants owners or seller, owners who are selling or sellers of companies worth between 10 and $40 million. We have to say that in the ad just exactly as Halbert did. Different style, because Ted's a different guy. But we have to be very clear about who we want. The lead generation magnet then is a free copy of Ted's book. And there's a website to go to to get it, where again, it discourages anybody who does not, the ad literally says, if you don't fit this criteria, we'd love to have you have the book if you want it, but go to Amazon and buy it. Don't come to us and get it for you. You're not playing fair, right? So there's nothing to do in that ad other than respond to the ad and get the book. So that's purely generation. At the opposite end is the no lead generation option, buy now only, come in, we got mattresses on sale. In the middle, you can play the hybrid game. And you can flip the emphasis either way. So for my financial advisors, for example, they live in a world where the only offer they wanna make, they're mostly sales culture guys, not marketing culture guys and gals. So they are of the, we eat today, what we kill today, conditioning. And so they don't like to, so their normal model is run ads for a week, bring people into workshop, do work, workshop, get to appointment, take money. And so the only offer they ever put in front of anybody is come into workshop, very high threshold offer. I know what's gonna happen to me, no matter what you tell me. I know you're not gonna feed me for free and teach me something and that's it. You're gonna lean on me to book an appointment, Susie's gonna bar the door, et cetera, et cetera. So I know what's gonna happen. I'm not sure who I'm gonna be sitting next to. I have to sort of expose myself. I'm 62 years old, so I'm still relatively civil and polite. So I'm reluctant to get up and walk out, which means I'm gonna be stuck there even if it's terrible for two hours, et cetera. So it's a very high threshold offer. So hybrid version A is PS. So we have a really cool free report, free DVDs for you about everything you need to know not to outlive your money, which everybody who comes to the workshop gets. If you are gonna be unable to come to the workshop or you are unwilling to come to the workshop, at least let us send you, and here's how to get that. So that's buy offer first, primary offer first, lead generation second, oh by the way. Now the minute you do that, you make every ad and marketing dollar work a little harder. So before you've ever sold anything, you just turn the dollar into a buck 20 or a buck 30 or a buck 40, it's magic. Right, okay, because we still got the ones. We're gonna come. Yeah, you may trade down a few, but what you pick up far trumps what you trade down. So we just made every ad dollar produce more because it filled our workshop and also filled our lead pipeline. So we maybe made a buck, well, dollar worth a buck 20. Well, you do that with enough dollars. Right. It adds up, right? The flip of that is, here's our free, everything you need to know not to outlive your money. And here's a full blown lead generation kind of pitch. Right, here's what's in it. It's only available for a limited time. Here's why it's free. Here's where to go get it. Oh by the way, we also have a workshop coming up in 10 days. And you are welcome to come to the workshop too. Right. And here's the website to go to, to learn about the workshop. That's the other hybrid approach. Got it, got it. And when people have, so like one of my financial advisor clients has a bought and paid for radio show. You see this a lot with finance, you see it a lot with health. Where they bought two hours on a radio every Saturday. It's a talk show, but they own it. And so they have, so let's say eight commercials within it. Right. Normally all their commercials are about next week's workshop. Now we divvy them up. Two commercials are about the workshop. Two commercials are about the doohickey of the month. The magnet. The magnet of the month. Right. Okay. Two of the commercials are hybrids. Okay. For the same radio show, for the same dollar, I just made the dollar worth about $2.10. It's the current math. Before we sell anything. Right. I just turned a dollar bill into $2.10. Wow, fantastic, fantastic. So let me go back to the media question. People say, okay, so how do I know what media I should be using to do this lead generation in? What's the best media is a question we often get? You wanna use, well, so first of all, there's no good or bad media. I mean, there's no such thing. Okay. For it's situational. It's always situational. So neither a hammer or a scalpel is a good tool or a bad tool. If you wanna drive a nail into the wall to hang a picture, a hammer's a better tool than a scalpel. Right. If you wanna do brain surgery, unless you're hitting Moe or Larry, chances are you're gonna prefer the scalpel. Right. Moe or Larry. So there's no. So you prefer Larry go to the disco quite a bit. Very, yeah, well. You're making fun of my references. So there is no good or bad media. And you can't have a bias getting your way about, for example, about being an offline person by nature conditioning or experience and just summarily rejecting online or vice versa. So it's all situational. And the second thing is you wanna use at least more than one because the worst number of business is one of anything. You should never sleep at night if you have one of anything because it can be taken away from you. It can become dysfunctional. It can, competition can destroy it. Et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So you definitely want more than one. You actually want as many as you can make work within your maximum allowable costs per lead and sale. So there's math to this. You have to decide, you have to tell me, most business owners don't already know this. They have not calculated this. And they have a difficult time with the calculation. But you have to decide what the most you will spend is to get a, let's use the word customer, patient, client, doctor, to get a customer. Then ultimately you'll be able to back up immediate to the maximum you're willing to spend per lead because you'll know how many leads it takes to convert from this source and so forth. But you start with the customer number, you get to the lead number. The higher that number is, the more power you have in the marketplace, which loops all the way back around to why price elasticity is important, profit's important, smart price strategy is important, good lead management and nurturing process so we don't waste leads. All that's critical because it allows us to pay more to get a customer. So the more you'll spend to get a customer, not stupidly you understand, then the more power you have. Then with that number, you will be able to find, you'll find some media you can't make perform within that number. You'll find some media barely get in under it. So let's say our number's 100 bucks. You'll find some media that will bring you a customer for $98.76. You'll find other media that'll bring you a customer for $22.50. Your dumb business person temptation will be, we're not gonna do the ones that bring us customers at 98 bucks, we're only gonna do the ones that bring customers at $22. Stupid for all sorts of reasons. First of all, we have an analyzed quality of customer differentiating, but that's really advanced. But it's stupid for a bunch of other reasons. One, greatest barrier to entry and discouragement to competitors is you're everywhere. So the more media you're using, the more discouraging you are to current and particularly to new competition. So you wanna be able to use all you can. Secondly, there's stability and diversity. So this one's up a little this month, this one's down a little this month, vice versa. So let's say TV works really great for you. Well, it'll work really great for you right up until about two months before the presidential election. Then it will cost you 12 times more. Or the next time we invade Iraq, because then everybody watches CNN for six weeks. If all you're doing is television, you're screwed, that's exactly right. That's the technical term for it. You are screwed. I watched the first invasion of Iraq. I was still doing a lot of work with Guthrie Wanker then. And we watched infomercial sales plummet from joyous numbers to zero almost overnight. Cause everybody's watching the live invasion of Iraq on CNN. They ain't clicking. We don't have a prayer of holding them for 30 minutes on the vacuum cleaner that picks up nails, right? It's over. So it's the second reason you don't wanna be very dependent on any one or on any few. You wanna use everything you can that comes in under your maximum allowable, dollar cost average, make that number work. That's what you wanna do. That allows you to do a lot of cool stuff that most business people don't think that they can do. You may be willing to have a maximum allowable that loses money on the first transaction. Depends on how close to capacity you are, what your growth philosophy is, all of that. So then you want multimedia integration. That's the next thing you want. And it's the other reason why you wanna use more than one cause you can't integrate one. But you can integrate six. So again, with my one client, he mailed last year, I don't know, give or take a half a million postcards. Four different postcards. They all drive to websites where there are video assisted sales letters. And then at the back end of those websites, there is direct mail. There is inbound telemarketing. And there is outbound telemarketing. Now there are free preview seminars all across the country. So there's a variety of means to take that lead and convert them to a customer and to take that customer and move him up. The same thing applies to dental practice. So maybe it's coming to the office for a free exam, free consultation, for you see what your mouth would look like after we did all the work. But there's also a place for the in-office seminar for 90 minutes for 25 people where everybody gets to ask their questions and all that in an informal environment. There's a place for the webcast for all the accumulated leads every two months who won't come physically. But at home, they'll too did it and ask questions. So multimedia integration. Now having said all of that, if you put the gun against my head and say, still go back to the question everybody wants to ask which is well, but tell me the one best, me. Right. I will default to direct mail more than I will anyplace else. It's the most predictable, manageable, reliable, formulaic, when you get a system in place, it can function for the longest period of time. And you can really target it. With the least change, you can tremendously target. So sort of as the direct mail dinosaur, I always delight in these sorts of things. This is our members will see it in the newsletter. But so this is a letter I got right before I did the newsletter. And I'll just, I use the version I put in the newsletter. So this is from a new gold member, also his name's Tony Zito. Well, so I was going to say, so the other thing to understand is most businesses actually create multiple lead pools that each deserve a magnetic marketing system, preferably a multimedia one. So a business may have visit site, no action. Right, they visited, they bailed. What do we do with them? They may have call, no appointment. Every professional practice has it. Every store has it.
